figures.caption: Table 1. Quantitative results on ScanNet++ [45] evaluating rendered depth accuracy and novel view synthesis. MDE variants are sorted in a descending order of δ1 accuracy (the second column) for monocular depth on all training views. The top-performing result (w.r.t. each depth prior) is highlighted in bold, and cases underperforming the baseline (3DGS without depth) are underlined.
Figure 5. Qualitative results on 3DGS. (Top): low-data setting; (Bottom): moderate-data setting.

Figure 6. Qualitative comparisons of rendered images and depth maps on the ScanNet++ dataset for the baseline (without depth supervision), Lsid, and our method using MoGe-2 monocular depth supervision. Our method produces clearer, more accurate depth maps and yields sharper, less artifact-prone renderings than the baseline and Lsid. We encourage readers to zoom in for a clearer visual comparison.
Table 3. Results on the MipNeRF 360. Best performance is bolded in red and the second is marked in orange.

Figure 2. Impact of SfM point count in scale alignment on GS rendering quality for identical training images and point clouds. When using only Lsid, fewer observed points lead to degraded performance compared to the baseline (without using monocular depth supervision), highlighting the limitation of scale-invariant depth supervision in aligning monocular depth cues.

Figure 4. Comparison of different relative depth supervision losses, shown on two zoomed-in scene crops. Gradient-based loss (GAL) achieves sharper rendering with fewer smearing artifacts compared to the patch-based Pearson correlation loss, as seen in the reduced distortion on photo-frame edges in the top row and a clearer shape around the orange triangular region in the bottom.

Figure 3. Overview of our proposed framework. During GS optimization, depth-inconsistent Gaussians are identified using a virtual stereo setup (Sec. 4.1). These regions guide the learning of absolute depth corrections from monocular depth priors aligned with sparse SfM point clouds. Additionally, a relative depth loss (Sec. 4.2) is applied to learn geometric cues under ambiguous scene scales.
